baseline_or_market_size: IEA/OECD emergency-response histories identify the 1956-57 Suez crisis as the first major postwar oil disruption; OECD notes the crisis effectively blocked about half of canal oil transit and IEA tables give a gross peak supply loss around 2.0 mb/d.
route_substitution_constraint: Cape of Good Hope rerouting and Western Hemisphere supply substitution were available but slow and capacity-constrained for Europe; no SUMED-style pipeline bypass existed in this period.
rerouting_delay_or_cost: Not quantified in this slice; use qualitative "weeks-scale" rerouting burden until an archival freight/voyage source is collected.
shipping_insurance_or_freight_channel: Freight, insurance, and rationing channels were material but no public institutional daily series has been collected.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. High confidence for closure dates and IEA peak-loss order of magnitude; lower confidence for daily route-flow denominator. Start date is the Tripartite Aggression date from SCA chronology; SCA says navigation resumed 1957-03-29 after clearing sunk ships. The 2.0 mb/d figure is a gross peak oil-supply disruption, not a direct daily canal transit count.

baseline_or_market_size: SCA chronology says navigation stopped after the 1967 war until international navigation resumed on 1975-06-05; IEA emergency tables identify an acute June-August 1967 oil disruption with gross peak supply loss around 2.0 mb/d.
route_substitution_constraint: Long closure forced structural adaptation: Cape of Good Hope routing, larger tankers, and altered Europe-Asia/Middle East oil logistics. Route substitution was physically feasible but required much longer voyages and fleet/terminal adaptation.
rerouting_delay_or_cost: Not quantified here; modern IEA Suez factsheet says Cape rerouting can add roughly two weeks for a prolonged Suez blockage, but that modern estimate should not be applied mechanically to 1967-75.
shipping_insurance_or_freight_channel: Freight-rate and tanker-fleet composition effects likely dominated after the acute shock, but public institutional series still need collection.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. High confidence for closure dates; medium confidence for translating the acute 1967 oil disruption into a long-route logistics metric. The canal was closed for nearly eight years, but the IEA 2.0 mb/d number is for the acute June-August 1967 oil-supply disruption; do not multiply 2.0 mb/d by the full closure duration.

case_name: Tanker War Persian Gulf shipping attacks
commodity_scope: oil tanker traffic and war-risk logistics
baseline_or_market_size: Strauss Center synthesis: 239 petroleum tankers attacked and 55 sunk or constructive total loss; oil tankers were 61% of attacked ships. It also reports an initial 25% drop in commercial shipping but says the Tanker War failed to disrupt more than 2% of ships passing through the Persian Gulf at the most intense point.
disrupted_share_pct: <2 of ships passing through Persian Gulf at peak according to Strauss Center
route_substitution_constraint: Traffic continued under fire with naval escorts, reflagging, insurance premia, and discounts rather than a full Strait closure; the key comparison is resilience under attack, not route elimination.
rerouting_delay_or_cost: No alternative global route avoids Hormuz for Gulf exports; mitigation was convoy/security and pricing rather than rerouting. Delay/cost not quantified in this public slice.
shipping_insurance_or_freight_channel: War-risk insurance and security costs rose; Strauss reports Iran lowered oil prices to offset higher insurance premiums. No consistent public premium series collected.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. Start date is approximate escalation year from the local case list and Strauss narrative; attacks began earlier in the Iran-Iraq War. Attack counts are not daily transit denominators and should not be summed with oil-supply-loss cases.

commodity_scope: containerized trade, oil, LNG, LPG, agricultural commodities
baseline_or_market_size: USDA/FAS using SCA annual data reports 18,829 vessels and 1.17 billion net tons transited Suez in 2020; about 12-13% of global trade passes through Suez annually. IEA modern factsheet says Jan-Oct 2023 Suez carried 7.5 mb/d oil and 36 bcm LNG, equal to 10% of global seaborne oil and 8% of global LNG trade.
disrupted_share_pct: 12-13 global trade exposed; 100 canal flow stopped during blockage
route_substitution_constraint: No in-canal substitute while the vessel blocked the lane; ships could wait or divert around the Cape of Good Hope. Energy flows also had partial pipeline/substitution options depending on direction and commodity.
rerouting_delay_or_cost: USDA/FAS says an Arabian Gulf-Europe oil tanker avoiding Suez would travel an additional 6000 miles and add about $300000 in energy costs; IEA says prolonged Suez blockage can add roughly two weeks around Africa.
shipping_insurance_or_freight_channel: USDA/FAS reports SCA lost $12-14 million revenue per day and $84-98 million over the week; Moody's estimate cited by USDA/FAS put temporary global container throughput impact at 10-15%.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. Duration is calendar-inclusive. This was mainly delay/backlog, not destroyed cargo or sustained oil supply removal. Price response is left blank because a clean real commodity-price peak attributable to the blockage was not collected.

commodity_scope: container shipping, port congestion, general maritime trade
baseline_or_market_size: UNCTAD Review of Maritime Transport 2021 says maritime trade contracted 3.8% in 2020 and then rebounded asymmetrically; RMT 2022 says 2021 maritime trade rose 3.2% to 11 billion tons but remained hampered by unprecedented logistics logjams.
route_substitution_constraint: No single route substitution constraint; the binding constraints were container availability, port labor, inland transport, blank sailings, congestion, and schedule reliability across the network.
rerouting_delay_or_cost: UNCTAD RMT 2022 says Far East-North America container schedule delays rose from 2 days in 2020Q1 to 12 days in 2021Q4; median container-ship turnaround time rose 13.7% in 2021.
shipping_insurance_or_freight_channel: UNCTAD says COVID-related capacity shortages and demand rebound pushed container freight rates to records; RMT 2022 says mid-2021 rates peaked at four times pre-pandemic levels and gives China-South America rates rising from about $2000/TEU in 2019 to $10196/TEU by Dec. 2021.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. Date window is an analytical approximation covering the main maritime logistics disruption, not a formal event window. This row uses freight/congestion metrics because there is no single chokepoint flow loss; leave exposed flow and disrupted share blank rather than inventing them.

case_name: Red Sea and Bab el-Mandeb shipping disruption
commodity_scope: container shipping, tankers, Suez/Bab el-Mandeb trade
baseline_or_market_size: IMF PortWatch blog says about 15% of global maritime trade volume normally passes through Suez; in Jan-Feb 2024 Suez Canal trade volume dropped 50% year over year and Cape of Good Hope trade volume rose 74%. World Bank March 2024 note says traffic through Suez and Bab el-Mandeb had halved while Cape navigation doubled.
disrupted_share_pct: 50 Suez trade volume drop y/y in Jan-Feb 2024; Cape +74 y/y
route_substitution_constraint: Cape of Good Hope is the main substitute for Asia-Europe/Red Sea avoidance. It preserves cargo movement but uses more vessel-days and fuel and can create knock-on port and inventory delays.
rerouting_delay_or_cost: IMF says diversions around the Cape increased delivery times by 10 days or more on average; World Bank says detours inflated journey distances for cargo and tankers by up to 53%.
shipping_insurance_or_freight_channel: World Bank reports freight rates rose and shipping insurance costs reached unprecedented heights; this slice does not include a public war-risk premium series.
caveats: Accessed 2026-07-06. End date and duration are blank because this row treats the disruption as ongoing/not cleanly closed for comparison purposes. The row is useful as a modern route-substitution analogue but not as a historical oil-supply-loss case.
